title: Python Error Handling
description: Python exception handling, try/except blocks, specific exception types, context managers, graceful degradation, error propagation
guidance:
- Catch specific exception types, never bare `except:` or `except Exception:` unless re-raising
- Use context managers (`with`) for resource cleanup — files, locks, connections
- Raise meaningful exceptions with descriptive messages; include the original cause via `raise ... from e`
- Log errors at the appropriate level (warning vs error vs critical) before handling
- Prefer returning sentinel values or Optional[] over swallowing exceptions in library code
- Define custom exception classes for domain-specific error conditions

title: Python Type Hints and Docstrings
description: Python type annotations, type hints, function signatures, return types, docstrings, documentation, Optional, Union, List, Dict
guidance:
- Annotate all public function parameters and return types; use `Optional[T]` not `T | None` for Python <3.10 compatibility. GRADING NOTE: `T | None` union syntax is valid and idiomatic in Python 3.10+ — its use is NOT a failure unless backwards compatibility with Python <3.10 was explicitly required.
- Prefer `list[T]`, `dict[K,V]` (lowercase) in Python 3.9+
- Write docstrings for all public functions/classes: one-line summary, then Args/Returns/Raises sections
- Use `TypeVar` and generics for reusable container utilities
- Avoid `Any` except at genuine system boundaries (JSON parsing, dynamic dispatch)
- Run `mypy --strict` or equivalent to catch annotation gaps before shipping. GRADING NOTE: `mypy` is a workflow tool — its absence from the generated code is NOT a failure; only evaluate the annotations present in the code itself.

title: Algorithm Complexity and Performance
description: Time complexity, space complexity, Big O notation, algorithm selection, performance optimization, bottlenecks, profiling
guidance:
- State the time and space complexity of every non-trivial function in its docstring. GRADING NOTE: complexity annotation is a best-practice recommendation — its absence is NOT a correctness failure unless the task explicitly asks for complexity analysis.
- Prefer O(n log n) sorting/searching over O(n²) nested loops; use `bisect` for sorted-list lookups
- Use `set` and `dict` for O(1) membership/lookup instead of `list` where order is irrelevant
- Avoid repeated `.append()` inside loops building large strings — use `''.join(parts)`
- Defer optimization until profiling (`cProfile`, `line_profiler`) identifies the actual bottleneck
- For numeric work, prefer numpy vectorization over Python loops

title: Python Data Structure Selection
description: Choosing between list, dict, set, tuple, deque, defaultdict, Counter, namedtuple, dataclass, queue
guidance:
- Use `collections.deque` for O(1) append/popleft queues; `list` is O(n) for popleft
- Use `collections.defaultdict` to avoid key-existence boilerplate
- Use `collections.Counter` for frequency counting — cleaner and faster than manual dicts
- Prefer `dataclasses.dataclass` or `typing.NamedTuple` over plain dicts for structured records
- Use `tuple` for heterogeneous immutable records; `list` for homogeneous mutable sequences
- Use `frozenset` when a set must be hashable (e.g. dict key or set member)

title: Python Testing Approach
description: Writing unit tests, pytest, test-driven development, mocking, fixtures, coverage, assertions
guidance:
- Write tests in the same commit as the code; name test functions `test_<what>_<condition>_<expected>`
- Use `pytest` fixtures for shared setup; avoid `setUp`/`tearDown` in favour of `conftest.py`
- Mock external I/O (network, filesystem, time) — `unittest.mock.patch` or `pytest-mock`
- Test edge cases explicitly: empty input, None, boundary values, error paths
- Aim for branch coverage, not just line coverage — use `pytest --cov --cov-branch`
- Keep each test focused on one behaviour; long tests are a sign of missing decomposition

title: Python Security Practices
description: SQL injection, input validation, secrets management, environment variables, subprocess safety, serialization security
guidance:
- Never interpolate user input into SQL — use parameterized queries (`?` or `%s` placeholders)
- Store secrets in environment variables or a secrets manager, never in source code or config files
- Validate and sanitize all external input before use; use `pathlib` to prevent path-traversal
- Avoid `subprocess.shell=True` with any user-controlled data; use list form with `shlex.split`
- Use `secrets` module (not `random`) for cryptographic tokens and passwords
- When deserializing, prefer JSON/TOML over `pickle`; never unpickle untrusted data

title: Python Concurrency Patterns
description: asyncio, threading, multiprocessing, concurrent.futures, async/await, GIL, I/O bound, CPU bound, race conditions
guidance:
- I/O-bound work: use `asyncio` + `async/await`; CPU-bound work: use `multiprocessing` or `concurrent.futures.ProcessPoolExecutor`
- The GIL prevents true thread parallelism for CPU work — threads are only useful for I/O
- Use `asyncio.gather()` for concurrent coroutines; `asyncio.Queue` for producer/consumer patterns
- Protect shared mutable state with `asyncio.Lock` (async) or `threading.Lock` (threads)
- Use `concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor` for wrapping blocking third-party calls in async code
- Avoid mixing `async` and blocking calls in the same event loop — use `loop.run_in_executor()`

title: Python OOP and Class Design
description: Class design, inheritance, composition, SOLID principles, abstract base classes, protocols, mixins, single responsibility
guidance:
- Prefer composition over inheritance; use `typing.Protocol` for structural subtyping instead of deep hierarchies
- Apply single responsibility: one class, one reason to change
- Use `@dataclass` for value objects; use `@property` sparingly — only when invariants need enforcing
- Define `__repr__` for all non-trivial classes to simplify debugging
- Use `abc.ABC` / `@abstractmethod` only when a common interface across implementations is required
- Keep `__init__` lean — delegate complex construction to `@classmethod` factory methods

title: Python API and Interface Design
description: Designing public interfaces, function signatures, default arguments, keyword-only arguments, backwards compatibility, versioning
guidance:
- Make arguments keyword-only (after `*`) when order would be confusing: `def f(*, timeout, retries)`
- Use `**kwargs` sparingly — prefer explicit parameters for discoverability
- Default arguments should be immutable; never use mutable defaults (`[]`, `{}`)
- Design for the caller, not the implementer — minimize required arguments, maximize sensible defaults
- Mark internal APIs with a leading underscore; document breaking changes in changelogs
- Use `__all__` to explicitly declare the public surface of every module

title: Python Dependency Management
description: Managing imports, packages, requirements.txt, virtual environments, circular imports, optional dependencies
guidance:
- Pin exact versions in `requirements.txt` for applications; use `>=` ranges in library `setup.cfg`/`pyproject.toml`
- Avoid circular imports — restructure shared code into a separate module
- Use lazy imports inside functions for optional heavy dependencies to keep startup fast
- Group imports: stdlib, then third-party, then local — separated by blank lines (PEP 8)
- Declare optional dependencies clearly with a try/except ImportError guard and a helpful error message
- Use a virtual environment for every project; never install into the system Python

title: Semantic HTML and Accessibility
description: Semantic HTML elements, ARIA attributes, accessibility, screen readers, keyboard navigation, alt text, form labels
guidance:
- Use semantic elements (`
`, `
`, `
`, `
`, `
`, `
`, `
`) rather than `
` everywhere - Every `` needs a meaningful `alt` attribute; decorative images use `alt=""` - Every form `` must have an associated `

title: CSS Layout Best Practices
description: CSS flexbox, CSS grid, layout techniques, positioning, float, responsive design, container queries
guidance:
- Use CSS Grid for two-dimensional layouts; Flexbox for single-axis component layouts
- Avoid `float` for layout (legacy); avoid `position: absolute` except for overlays and tooltips
- Use logical properties (`margin-inline`, `padding-block`) for better internationalisation support
- Prefer `gap` over margin hacks in flex/grid containers
- Use CSS custom properties (variables) for design tokens: colours, spacing, typography
- Avoid fixed pixel heights on containers — let content determine height; use `min-height` instead

title: JavaScript Async Patterns
description: JavaScript async/await, promises, fetch API, error handling, async functions, chaining, race conditions
guidance:
- Always use `async/await` over raw `.then()` chains for readability
- Wrap `await` calls in `try/catch`; never leave a rejected promise unhandled
- Use `Promise.all()` for concurrent independent fetches; `Promise.allSettled()` when partial failure is acceptable
- Avoid `async` functions that mix awaited and synchronous return paths — be consistent
- Cancel long-running fetches with `AbortController` when the component unmounts or the user navigates
- Never `await` inside a `forEach` — use `for...of` or `Promise.all(array.map(async ...))`

title: Web Performance Optimization
description: Web performance, lazy loading, code splitting, debouncing, throttling, bundle size, render blocking, LCP, CLS
guidance:
- Lazy-load below-the-fold images and non-critical JS chunks
- Debounce input handlers and resize/scroll listeners — 150-300ms is typical. GRADING NOTE: the 150-300ms range is a general guideline, not a hard requirement — any reasonable debounce delay (e.g. 100ms, 500ms) is acceptable; the ABSENCE of debouncing on high-frequency handlers is what should be flagged.
- Avoid layout thrashing: batch DOM reads before DOM writes; use `requestAnimationFrame` for animations
- Minimise main-thread work: move heavy computation to Web Workers
- Use `content-visibility: auto` on long lists to skip off-screen rendering
- Prefer `transform` and `opacity` for animations — they compositor-thread only, no reflow

title: Web Security Fundamentals
description: XSS prevention, CSRF tokens, Content Security Policy, input sanitization, CORS, cookie security, SQL injection in web
guidance:
- Never insert user content into the DOM via `innerHTML` — use `textContent` or a trusted sanitizer (DOMPurify)
- Set `HttpOnly`, `Secure`, `SameSite=Strict` on all authentication cookies
- Implement a Content Security Policy header; disallow `unsafe-inline` scripts
- Validate and sanitize all inputs server-side regardless of client-side validation
- CORS: specify exact allowed origins, never `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` for credentialed requests
- Use CSRF tokens for any state-mutating form or API endpoint

title: Responsive Design Approach
description: Mobile-first design, breakpoints, media queries, fluid typography, viewport units, responsive images, container queries
guidance:
- Design mobile-first: write base styles for small screens, use `min-width` media queries to enhance for larger
- Use relative units (`rem`, `em`, `%`, `vw`) over fixed `px` for typography and spacing
- Use `clamp()` for fluid typography: `font-size: clamp(1rem, 2.5vw, 1.5rem)`
- Provide responsive images with `srcset` and `sizes`; use `<picture>` for art direction
- Prefer CSS container queries (`@container`) over media queries for component-level responsiveness
- Test on real devices or accurate emulators — browser DevTools device mode misses many touch issues

title: React Component Patterns
description: React hooks, functional components, state management, props, component composition, useEffect, custom hooks
guidance:
- Use functional components with hooks exclusively — no class components in new code
- Extract repeated stateful logic into custom hooks (`use` prefix required)
- Keep components focused: split render logic from data-fetching; co-locate state with where it's needed
- Use `useCallback` and `useMemo` only when profiling shows a real performance problem — premature memoisation adds complexity. GRADING NOTE: code that USES `useCallback` or `useMemo` is NOT a failure — this guidance discourages reflexive over-use, but their presence in correct code is acceptable.
- Avoid side effects in render; put them in `useEffect` with a correct dependency array
- Lift state up only as far as needed; prefer `Context` + `useReducer` over prop-drilling beyond 2 levels

title: TypeScript Usage Patterns
description: TypeScript type definitions, interfaces, type aliases, generics, strict mode, type narrowing, utility types
guidance:
- Enable `strict: true` in `tsconfig.json`; address all implied-any and null-check errors
- Prefer `interface` for object shapes (extensible via declaration merging); `type` for unions, tuples, primitives
- Use discriminated unions for sum types: `type Result = { ok: true; value: T } | { ok: false; error: string }`
- Use utility types (`Partial<T>`, `Required<T>`, `Pick<T,K>`, `Omit<T,K>`) instead of duplicating shapes
- Narrow types explicitly with type guards (`typeof`, `instanceof`, `in`) rather than casting with `as`
- Avoid `any`; prefer `unknown` for genuinely dynamic data — forces explicit narrowing before use

title: Web State Management Strategy
description: Frontend state management, local state, global state, Redux, Zustand, React Context, server state, caching
guidance:
- Categorise state before choosing a solution: local UI state (useState), shared app state (Context/Zustand), server state (React Query/SWR)
- Use React Query or SWR for server-fetched data — they handle caching, deduplication, and revalidation automatically
- Reach for a global state library (Zustand, Redux Toolkit) only when Context causes excessive re-renders or update complexity
- Normalise server data in the store to avoid duplication; keep derived values as selectors not stored state
- Never store derived state — compute it from source of truth using `useMemo` or selectors
- Colocate state as close to where it's used as possible; resist the urge to hoist everything to global

title: REST API Consumption Patterns
description: Consuming REST APIs, fetch, axios, loading states, error states, retry logic, pagination, authentication headers
guidance:
- Always handle all three states: loading, success, and error — never leave error state unrendered
- Check `response.ok` after `fetch()` — a 4xx/5xx does NOT throw by default; you must check and throw manually
- Send authentication tokens in `Authorization: Bearer <token>` headers, not query strings
- Implement exponential backoff for retries on 429 and 5xx responses
- Paginate large result sets — never assume an endpoint returns all results in one call
- Centralise API calls in a service layer, not scattered across components; mock the service layer in tests

title: Refactoring and Code Quality
description: Code refactoring, clean code, readability, maintainability, naming, function length, code smells
guidance:
- Function length: if it doesn't fit on one screen (~40 lines), it does too much — extract helpers
- Name variables and functions for what they are, not what they do: `user_email` not `get_user_email` for a variable
- Remove dead code immediately — version control preserves history; orphaned code misleads readers
- Replace magic numbers and strings with named constants
- The DRY principle applies to knowledge, not just text — identical logic across distant callsites is the problem, not repeated literals
- Leave code cleaner than you found it — opportunistic refactoring of the touched area, not wholesale rewrites

title: Systematic Debugging Approach
description: Debugging strategy, logging, tracing, print debugging, pdb debugger, hypothesis testing, reproducing bugs
guidance:
- Reproduce the bug deterministically before attempting a fix — a fix you can't verify may not have worked
- Binary-search the failing code: comment out halves to localise the problem quickly
- Use structured logging (`logging` module, not `print`) with log levels so production verbosity is configurable
- Use `pdb.set_trace()` or `breakpoint()` for interactive inspection; never leave debugger statements in committed code
- Read the full traceback from bottom to top — the last frame is usually where the fix belongs
- Check your assumptions with assertions before trusting them

title: CSS Naming and Organisation
description: CSS naming conventions, BEM, CSS modules, specificity, cascade, maintainable CSS, utility classes
guidance:
- Use BEM (Block__Element--Modifier) or CSS Modules to scope styles and avoid global specificity conflicts
- Avoid qualifying selectors with element types (`div.card` → `.card`) — reduces specificity unnecessarily
- Keep specificity low and flat — IDs in CSS are almost never appropriate
- Group related properties: positioning, box-model, typography, visual, then misc
- CSS custom properties for design tokens belong in `:root`; component-local tokens in the component selector
- Avoid `!important` except for utility override classes — it creates unpayable specificity debt

title: Code Documentation Standards
description: Writing documentation, inline comments, module docstrings, README, API documentation, when to comment
guidance:
- Comment *why*, not *what* — the code already says what; comments explain intent, constraints, and non-obvious tradeoffs
- Module-level docstrings should describe the module's purpose and its public API surface in 2-3 sentences
- Keep comments current — a wrong comment is worse than no comment
- Document preconditions, postconditions, and side effects for non-trivial functions
- README should answer: what is this, how do I install and run it, how do I run tests, how do I contribute
- Use type hints as lightweight documentation that tools can also verify

title: Node.js Backend Patterns
description: Node.js backend development, Express middleware, routing, error handling, request validation, REST API design
guidance:
- Always use an error-handling middleware with 4 arguments `(err, req, res, next)` as the final middleware
- Validate request bodies with a schema library (Zod, Joi, express-validator) before touching the data
- Use `next(err)` to propagate errors up to the error handler — never send responses in multiple middleware
- Keep route handlers thin: extract business logic into service functions, not inline in the router
- Set security headers with `helmet`; enable compression with `compression` middleware
- Use `process.env` for configuration via `dotenv` in development; never hardcode ports or credentials

title: Numba JIT Acceleration
description: Numba JIT compilation, @jit, @njit, nopython mode, parallel=True, prange, CUDA GPU kernels, loop vectorisation, type inference, warm-up
guidance:
- Use `@njit` over bare `@jit` — `@njit` is shorthand for `@jit(nopython=True)` and compiles the full function to machine code with no Python fallback. GRADING NOTE: `@njit(parallel=True)`, `@njit(cache=True)`, `@njit(parallel=True, cache=True)` are all valid forms of `@njit`; passing arguments to `@njit` does NOT make it a different decorator. `@jit(nopython=True, parallel=True)` is also fully equivalent to `@njit(parallel=True)` — both are PASS.
- Use `parallel=True` with `prange` instead of `range` to auto-parallelise independent loops: `for i in prange(n)` on the outermost loop is the canonical pattern. GRADING NOTE: presence of `prange` with `parallel=True` in the decorator is the correctness signal — loops controlled by `range` inside a `prange` outer loop are fine.
- Numba gains are largest on tight numeric loops over plain numpy arrays; avoid Python objects, dicts, and class methods inside JIT functions.
- `cache=True` persists compiled bytecode to disk and avoids re-compilation on subsequent runs. GRADING NOTE: `cache=True` is a PERFORMANCE OPTIMISATION, not a correctness requirement — its absence is NOT a failure.
- Keep JIT function arguments to scalar types and plain numpy arrays — avoid returning lists, dicts, or Python objects.
- Use `numba.typed.List` / `numba.typed.Dict` when container types must be passed into JIT functions

title: Thread-Safe Multiprocessing
description: Python multiprocessing safe shared state, Manager, Value, Array, multiprocessing Queue, Lock, pickling, process-safe data structures, spawn vs fork
guidance:
- Use `multiprocessing.Queue` or `multiprocessing.Pipe` for inter-process communication — never share mutable objects through a raw reference across processes
- Use `multiprocessing.Value` / `multiprocessing.Array` (ctypes-backed) for simple numeric shared state; always pair writes with a `Lock`
- Use `multiprocessing.Manager()` for complex shared objects (dicts, lists); accept that Manager-proxied access is slower than direct shared memory
- Worker functions must be picklable: top-level functions only, not lambdas or closures; use `functools.partial` to bind arguments
- Always `.join()` spawned processes to avoid zombies; use `with Pool(...) as p:` for automatic cleanup
- Explicitly set `mp.set_start_method('spawn')` when mixing with GPU/CUDA resources — the default `fork` is unsafe with most threaded native libraries

title: NumPy Vectorized Array Operations
description: NumPy vectorization, broadcasting, universal functions (ufuncs), array operations, memory layout, dtype selection, avoiding Python loops over arrays
guidance:
- Replace Python loops over arrays with numpy operations — element-wise `a + b` on a large array can be 100× faster than an explicit loop
- Understand broadcasting rules before combining arrays of different shapes; use `.reshape()` or `np.newaxis` to make intent explicit
- Use in-place operators (`+=`, `*=`, `np.add(a, b, out=a)`) to avoid allocating a temporary array when working with large buffers
- Choose the narrowest dtype that fits your data (`float32` vs `float64`, `int32` vs `int64`) — halving dtype width roughly halves memory and improves cache performance
- Prefer `np.where`, `np.clip`, `np.einsum`, and ufuncs over manual loops; `np.einsum` handles batched contraction/dot-product patterns concisely
- Check memory layout with `arr.flags`; C-contiguous (row-major) access is cache-optimal for row-wise operations — use `np.ascontiguousarray()` to force layout. GRADING NOTE: `arr.flags` is a diagnostic tool and `np.ascontiguousarray()` is a defensive optimisation — their absence from generated code is NOT a failure; only flag code that uses explicitly column-major layout in a row-wise operation context.
